request.question: Does Empire of the Sun or 3 Doors Down have more band members?

title: Empire of the Sun (band)
Bigger Than Life Tour
3 Doors Down (album)
Citizen/Soldier
Away from the Sun
3 Doors Down
Here Without You
When I'm Gone (3 Doors Down song)
The Doors discography
Seventeen Days
sentences: Empire of the Sun is an Australian electronic music duo from Sydney, formed in 2007.
The band is a collaboration between Luke Steele of alternative rock act The Sleepy Jackson, and Nick Littlemore of electronic dance outfit Pnau.
Their 2008 debut album "Walking on a Dream" brought the duo international success and has been certified double platinum in Australia and gold in the United Kingdom.

The album provided a number of internationally charting singles including "Walking on a Dream" which peaked at number ten on the Australian ARIA Singles Chart and reached number sixty four on the UK Singles Chart, and "We Are the People", which peaked at number fourteen on the UK Singles Chart.
The Bigger Than Life Tour was a co- headlining tour by American rock bands, Daughtry and 3 Doors Down.
The tour supported Daughtry's third studio album, "Break the Spell", and 3 Doors Down's "The Greatest Hits" album.
3 Doors Down is the self-titled fourth studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on May 20, 2008. "
It's Not My Time" was the first single from the album and was released in February 2008.
It can be heard on the band's MySpace site.
Another song on the album, "Citizen/Soldier" was released in 2007 as a tribute to the National Guard.
"Citizen/Soldier" is a single by the American alternative rock band 3 Doors Down from their self-titled album "3 Doors Down".
The song was released as a single in November 2007 in conjunction with a recruitment campaign by the United States National Guard.
The lyrics convey the band's views regarding the actions performed by the Guard.
It was released as the third single for American active rock and mainstream rock radio stations in November 2008, while it is their fourth overall single.
Away from the Sun is the second studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on November 12, 2002.
The singles released for the album were as follows: "When I'm Gone", "The Road I'm On", "Here Without You" and "Away from the Sun".
The recording sessions took place during the summer of 2002 with Producer/Engineer Rick Parashar at London Bridge Studios, in Seattle, Washington.
Rush guitarist Alex Lifeson performed live with the band during their premiere party in Biloxi for "Away from the Sun".
The album has sold four million copies worldwide, including well over three million in the US alone.
3 Doors Down is an American rock band from Escatawpa, Mississippi, that formed in 1996.
The band originally consisted of Brad Arnold (lead vocals/drums), Todd Harrell (bass), and Matt Roberts (lead guitar, backing vocals).
They were soon joined by rhythm guitarist Chris Henderson.
The band rose to international fame with their first single, "Kryptonite", which charted in the top three on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart.
The band then signed with Republic Records and released their debut album, "The Better Life", in 2000.
The album was the 11th-best-selling album of the year and was certified 6x platinum in the United States.
They were later joined by drummer Richard Liles, who played during the tour for their first album.
"Here Without You" is a song by American rock band 3 Doors Down.
It was released in August 2003 as the third single from the album "Away from the Sun".
It peaked at No. 5 on the "Billboard" Hot 100, for the week ending November 8, 2003.
Only their songs "Kryptonite" and "When I'm Gone" reached higher positions on the chart, peaking at No. 3 and No. 4, respectively.
It has since been certified 2x Platinum in the United States and Platinum in Australia.
The song was a large success on pop radio, becoming their third No. 1 hit on the Mainstream Top 40 chart and their first No. 1 on the Adult Top 40 chart, where it stayed at the top for 13 weeks.
"When I'm Gone" is a song by American rock band 3 Doors Down.
It was released in October 2002 as the lead single from their second album, "Away from the Sun".
It peaked at  4 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart in April 2003.
The song also spent seventeen weeks atop the "Billboard" Hot Mainstream Rock Tracks chart from November 2002 through March 2003, becoming one of the longest-running number-one singles on that chart.
The song also peaked  2 on the Hot Modern Rock Tracks chart almost reaching  1 by one spot from Queens of the Stone Age's "No One Knows" on the issue dated February 15, 2003.
It spent one week atop the Top 40 Mainstream chart for one week on May 3, 2003 and also peaked at  3 on the Adult Top 40 chart.
The following is the discography of the American rock band The Doors.
Formed in Los Angeles in 1965, the group consisted of Jim Morrison (vocals), Ray Manzarek (keyboards), John Densmore (drums), and Robby Krieger (guitar).
The Doors became one of the most popular rock bands of their era.
Their debut album, "The Doors" (1967), released by Elektra Records, charted at No. 2 on the US "Billboard" 200 and produced the group's most successful single, "Light My Fire".

The album received several sales certifications including a 4 times multi-platinum from both the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA), and from the Canadian Recording Industry Association (CRIA).

The Doors' second studio album, "Strange Days" (1967), often recognized as their most creative output, failed to produce a hit single as popular as "Light My Fire", though the album sold well commercially but did not reach the same level of success as the debut.
It reached No. 3 on the "Billboard" 200 and was certified platinum in the United States and Canada.
The Doors' third studio album "Waiting for the Sun" (1968), was regarded as an artistic disappointment when compared to their earlier material.
However, commercially it was very successful and reached No. 1 in the US and France, and produced their second No. 1 single, "Hello, I Love You".
"Waiting for the Sun" was the first Doors album to chart in the United Kingdom, where it peaked inside the Top 20.
The album was certified gold in that country by the British Phonographic Industry (BPI), as well as being certified gold and platinum in several other countries.
Seventeen Days is the third studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on February 8, 2005, five years to the day that 3 Doors Down released their debut album, "The Better Life".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Does Empire of the Sun or 3 Doors Down have more band members?
hop1_docs: Another 700 Miles | Another 700 Miles is a live EP by 3 Doors Down released on November 11, 2003.  It was recorded live at a performance in Chicago, Illinois during the band's Away from the Sun tour.

High Tyde | High Tyde are an English indie pop quartet from Brighton, England.  The ages of the band members range from 18 to 19.  They have played at major music festivals, such as Boardmasters, Dot 2 Dot, Underground, Fieldview, Reading, and Y Not.  They have also played support shows for Little Comets, Bad Suns, Young Kato, and Peace.  They have been featured on BBC Radio 1.  The sound of the band is inspired from indie bands like Two Door Cinema Club and Foals.  Their sound also has similarities of bands like The 1975 and Bombay Bicycle Club.  Their music gives off a summer vibe.

Away from the Sun | Away from the Sun is the second studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on November 12, 2002.  The singles released for the album were as follows: "When I'm Gone", "The Road I'm On", "Here Without You" and "Away from the Sun".  The recording sessions took place during the summer of 2002 with Producer/Engineer Rick Parashar at London Bridge Studios, in Seattle, Washington.  Rush guitarist Alex Lifeson performed live with the band during their premiere party in Biloxi for "Away from the Sun".  The album has sold four million copies worldwide, including well over three million in the US alone.

3 Doors Down (album) | 3 Doors Down is the self-titled fourth studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on May 20, 2008. " It's Not My Time" was the first single from the album and was released in February 2008.  It can be heard on the band's MySpace site.  Another song on the album, "Citizen/Soldier" was released in 2007 as a tribute to the National Guard.

The Road I'm On | "The Road I'm On" is a song recorded by American rock band 3 Doors Down.  It was released in April 2003 as the second single from their second album, "Away from the Sun".  The song reached number 8 on the U.S. "Billboard" Mainstream Rock charts and number 24 on the U.S. "Billboard" Modern Rock charts.

Supralateral arc | A supralateral arc is a comparatively rare member of the halo family which in its complete form appears as a large, faintly rainbow-colored band in a wide arc above the sun and appearing to encircle it, at about twice the distance as the familiar 22° halo.  In reality, however, the supralateral arc does not form a circle and never reaches below the sun.  When present, the supralateral arc touches the (much more common) circumzenithal arc from below.  As in all colored halos, the arc has its red side directed towards the sun, its blue part away from it.

Walking on a Dream (song) | "Walking on a Dream" is a song by Australian electronic music duo Empire of the Sun, which was released on 30 August 2008 as the lead single ahead of their debut album of the same name (3 October 2008).  The track was co-written by band members, Luke Steele and Nick Littlemore with Jonathan "Donnie" Sloan.  The full Sam La More remix was made available as a free download on the band's official website.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- 3 Doors Down — [1] "Another 700 Miles is a live EP by 3 Doors Down" ; [3] "second studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down" ; [5] "\"The Road I'm On\" is a song recorded by American rock band 3 Doors Down."
- Empire of the Sun — [7] "\"Walking on a Dream\" is a song by Australian electronic music duo Empire of the Sun" ; [7] "band members, Luke Steele and Nick Littlemore" ; [7] "30 August 2008"

Ambiguities:
- "Empire of the Sun": passage [7] calls them a "duo" and names "Luke Steele and Nick Littlemore", but also mentions "Jonathan \"Donnie\" Sloan" (co-writer), so it is unclear whether any additional official or touring members exist beyond the duo.
- "3 Doors Down": passages mention the band and albums/songs but do not list band members or member count; lineup and historical membership could vary.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing explicit, authoritative counts of band members for both bands (current official membership and whether to include touring/collaborating musicians). Specifically: number of members in "3 Doors Down" and confirmation of the official number of members in "Empire of the Sun" (beyond the named duo).

Retrieval direction:
- Open the main band pages: "3 Doors Down" and "Empire of the Sun".
- Search/query: "3 Doors Down band members" or "How many members are in 3 Doors Down".
- Search/query: "Empire of the Sun band members Luke Steele Nick Littlemore duo" or "Empire of the Sun members".
- If needed, check "Empire of the Sun (band)" Wikipedia page and "3 Doors Down (band)" Wikipedia page for current and historical lineup.
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
response.hop2_query: Empire of the Sun 3 Doors Down members
hop2_docs: Another 700 Miles | Another 700 Miles is a live EP by 3 Doors Down released on November 11, 2003.  It was recorded live at a performance in Chicago, Illinois during the band's Away from the Sun tour.

Away from the Sun | Away from the Sun is the second studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on November 12, 2002.  The singles released for the album were as follows: "When I'm Gone", "The Road I'm On", "Here Without You" and "Away from the Sun".  The recording sessions took place during the summer of 2002 with Producer/Engineer Rick Parashar at London Bridge Studios, in Seattle, Washington.  Rush guitarist Alex Lifeson performed live with the band during their premiere party in Biloxi for "Away from the Sun".  The album has sold four million copies worldwide, including well over three million in the US alone.

The Road I'm On | "The Road I'm On" is a song recorded by American rock band 3 Doors Down.  It was released in April 2003 as the second single from their second album, "Away from the Sun".  The song reached number 8 on the U.S. "Billboard" Mainstream Rock charts and number 24 on the U.S. "Billboard" Modern Rock charts.

Two Doors Down | "Two Doors Down" was a song written and performed by Dolly Parton, which provided a 1978 U.S. country and pop hit for her.  The song was sung from the perspective of a woman who has just broken up with her boyfriend and is debating attending a party two doors down the hall from her apartment.  She decides to go, meets a new man and returns with him to her own apartment "two doors down".
To the Door of the Sun (Alle Porte del Sole) | "To the Door of the Sun (Alle Porte del Sole)" is a 1974 hit single by Al Martino.  It was the title track of his LP.

Banging Down the Doors | Banging Down the Doors is the first officially released album by Ezra Furman & The Harpoons.  It was released in 2007.  The titles on the album are largely the same as those originally recorded and self-released by the band as "Beat Beat Beat", but they were all re-recorded for "Banging Down the Doors".

The Sun Goes Down (Living It Up) | "The Sun Goes Down (Living It Up)" is a single released in 1983 by the musical group Level 42.  It is one of the few songs by the group which featured Mike Lindup on lead vocals (although Mark King did contribute as lead vocalist on this track).  After the success of "The Chinese Way", and of the album "The Pursuit of Accidents", this song featured on the newly released "Standing in the Light" album.  The first single from the album, "Out Of Sight, Out Of Mind", did not obtain the expected success for the recording and for the members of the band, leaving the whole expectation for the second single, The Sun Goes Down.
hop2_titles: To the Door of the Sun (Alle Porte del Sole)
The Sun Goes Down (Living It Up)
response.summary_2: Empire of the Sun is an Australian electronic music duo consisting of Luke Steele and Nick Littlemore; the provided passages give no clear member count for 3 Doors Down.
